column18:
Actually, I usedWindsurf has many better features than Cursor.
However, there's one aspect where it falls short.
Cursor has a summurize function when token usage reaches its maximum.
This function summarizes the conversation results and drastically reduces token usage.
Windsurf seems to have this feature, but it doesn't seem as robust as Cursor. It's also unclear whether it summurizes or simply deletes previous chat history.
Adding this feature would be a huge help to users.
Thank you to the Windsurf development team. cursor before.

The jetbrains plugin routinely stops responding to all clicks and keystrokes until I close the IDE and open it again. Speaking of continuing, the "continue" function after too much processing is really stupid. I never know if my prompt will take 8 credits or 16, and that's a problem.

column15: Devin is an autonomous AI software engineer agent that can work inside a development environment and complete coding tasks end-to-end. I haven’t used it personally, but mostly because as I’ve used agents to help me write code, I prefer to give the agents smaller tasks and then I review the code (in chunks) and often fix mistakes it leaves behind. So I’m worried about giving the keys to a fully automated system that can make these types of decisions and stuff without me. I’m not ready to say no to it yet though. Need more input.
column18:
Yes. I want to personally thank the dev team at Windsurf. I’m sure all you hear all day is either complaints, feature requests, shareholder gripes, or blind praises without any merit. Well, I want to say “Thank You”. Thank you for this amazing platform. I see the [virtually] daily updates. I read through all the change logs. And yeah, not every feature is going to be a home run. But when things come out like the “plan” feature… grand slam. I use that all the time when I’m starting something new.

What’s more though, is that as a senior software engineer I find that Windsurf makes coding for me so much faster.

Do the agents make mistakes? Oh you bet they do, but we learn how to work around them and adapt our agent prompts.  I do wish things like the rules actually worked (they don’t. At least I’ve never gotten them to work… could be user error though). That was a feature request I put in over a year ago (specifically asking for it to copy the concept of cursor rules, but used in markdown format) and it got a ton of upvotes. But what you ended up implementing was far superior to what I asked for. Again, I just wish it actually worked.

Seriously though, I don’t mind correcting the agent all the time (reviewing its code and asking it to use better coding methodologies, smarter loops, whatever)… I just treat it like a mid/junior developer who is doing a pull request… the difference is that it can write code at the speed of 50 junior devs.

Sorry, I’m rambling…

How about this for a sound bite. Please pass this on to your devs:

Thank you. Thank you for the late nights, the innovation, and the foresight to give us tools we never knew we needed. We see you, and we appreciate you.

Jeff Coughlin

For speed I put 4/5 because I feel like the tab autocomplete is quite slow but Windsurf's proprietary SWE 1.5 model is extremely fast. I have never used Cursor or its tab autocomplete so I can only compare to Antigravity, which I also think is slow.

I put 5 for quality of AI code because I feel like the dominating terms in that equation are the human and the model rather than the application layer scaffold. As far as Windsurf goes, features like fastcontext, plan mode, and codemaps, do wonders in giving engineers the tools to increase both quality AND velocity.
column15: I use it through Deepwiki.
column18:
I'm a very big fan of the SWE series of models but I'm not sure if the plan is to always offer them for free to premium users. 

Even if they are not free (I'm not talking about the "fast" offering), I would be glad to spend some fractional amount of prompt tokens where it's clearly still one of the cheapest offerings.

To be clear the fact that SWE is offered for free is not the main reason I'm a fan. The fact that they are fast and cheap is a factor.

I am a huge fan of fast context and codemaps. In general, I love Windsurf. I feel like the product is legit and built by killers. I am not a fan of Cursor's push toward hugely autonomous agents. I was not surprised to find out that their agent-built web browser does not compile.

Today Typescript open source repositories are filled with unfortunately sloppy pull requests. But pretty much everybody has access to the latest models. Proper use of features like Codemaps and Fastcontext actually help in increasing both quality AND velocity in a way that I feel a smarter model cannot.

Good luck.

column12: There are some frustrations but none sufficiently important that I can name right now.

This is a known issue with how the IDE edit tools interact with the filesystem on this environment. Files created or modified via the `write_to_file` / `edit` / `multi_edit` tools get placed into an IDE virtual buffer but sometimes don't flush to the actual Linux filesystem. The files appear in IDE tabs (so you can see the code) but `ls`, `cat`, `tree`, and build tools can't find them.

## Root Cause

The IDE's file write layer and the underlying OS filesystem are out of sync. This likely happens because:
- The IDE holds writes in memory and batches disk flushes
- On a remote EC2 instance, the IDE ↔ filesystem bridge may have latency or reliability gaps
- Ctrl+S doesn't help because the file was never created on disk in the first place — the IDE can't "save" to a path that doesn't exist at the OS level

## Reliable Workaround

**Always use `run_command` with `cat > file << 'EOF'` for file creation** instead of `write_to_file` or `edit` tools. Shell writes go directly to the filesystem and are immediately visible to `ls`, `node`, `vite`, etc.

For **edits to existing files**, `sed -i` or shell-based writes are also more reliable than the IDE edit tools on this environment.

## Going Forward

I'll adopt this as my default approach for this workspace:
1. **New files** → `cat > path << 'HEREDOC'` via `run_command`
2. **Edits** → `sed -i` or full file rewrite via shell when the change is significant
3. **Always verify** with `ls -la` or `wc -l` after writing
4. Use `edit` / `multi_edit` only for small, targeted changes to files already confirmed on disk

This should eliminate the persistence issue going forward. Want me to capture this as a workflow file in `.windsurf/workflows/` so it persists across sessions?

column18: In my view of the world, Cursor came out early and owned AI based IDE early, then came Windsurf which was a scrapy upstart doing the same thing.  They battled for awhile, and then came Antigravity and blew both out of the water.  Then came Claude Code and Codex, both of which make the first gen Cursor and Windsurf feel like child's play.  Cursor 2.0 came out to catch up, but Windsurf has been lagging behind.  Windsurf tries to play catchup constantly, but isn't pushing boundaries at all.  Is very capable, but will die a slow painful death doing what it's currently doing.  I'm honestly praying that Windsurf does something dramatic to turn things around and compete with the others pushing boundaries.  Devin has always been a curiosity on the side.  I've not seen a single headline covering Devin ever, and Windsurf has falled off a cliff in the press department as well.  Hoping that this comes together in a really powerful workflow some how.  Frankly, my biggest pain point at the moment is with UI/UX design.  All of these tools are super capable of building apps, but they all suck at UI/UX design. column12:
Seems you are trying to save tokens where you can, which I often see backfire. For example, when running scripts it will grep or tail, often missing the crucial part it needs, and end up running 5 or 10 times, using even more tokens than it needed to.

Recently, I was using windsurf with Claude sonnet 4.5, and due to poor grepping it ran a terraform command that destroyed my production infrastructure.

Also, the pricing displayed for each model can be wrong. I was using Opus on a promotional 2x pricing, and suddenly all my credits were gone. It still said 2x, but was charging me 6x. After an update and restart it showed correctly. When models change pricing or promos end, the user should have to confirm.

After using CLI level inference for models like CODEX / CLAUDE competitors like cursor I can say that Windsurf is good but can be slow and does tend to have a higher error rate than agentic models. I don't quite know where the bottleneck is but CLI level models tend to hit fewer roadblocks with understand a project and the intention where the windsurf model can fail on similar project instructions. It can be prompt related but that is point, more extensive prompt coordination is required to get IDE systems to the same level of competence (maybe system level prompt bloat?). Speed is an issue to with read / write being slower at the IDE level. You have a very good product though and at the price point it's hard to beat for MOST users and I think that's underrated, Cursor offers a slightly faster experience at significantly higher price points.

In addition, maybe there could be a mode in cascade that we can use to describe what we want to do and cascade can provide best fit LLM to use along with pros and cons of the suggested AI's.

I am a solo "older" (seasoned ?) developer that builds s/w for me to use on my farm instead of paying $$$ for other online services that almost fits what I need to do.  With windsurf, I can write a Django web app faster than using excel and it is much easier than using excel.  I can also build exactly what I need to do and I can manage my own data.  I host my sites on Linode.  Unlike Excel, I can then access my site from anywhere on my farm from my phone/tablet.

Here is how I use windsurf.
I will use a higher credit cost thinking AI to do the upfront planning and initial system implementation, then I will use a lower credit cost, or free llm, to implement smaller changes that I need to make like simple refactoring.  For larger changes/refactoring I will switch to a higher credit cost thinking model.

I really enjoy using windsurf.  I spent most of my career writing back end server software (GE Medical, AGFA Healthcare) but not front end.  I do not like doing the front end javascript, html, etc.  coding.  Windsurf and AI takes care of the the front end UI stuff so I can focus on the domain and what the app should be doing for me.

column18:
I really enjoy your product. It has come a long way in the last year. The ability for the models to understand the context of the project—so they don't just try to create new things—has been very nice.

For example, I am the CTO of a company, but I come from a non-technical background. Although I have a dev team, there was a period of time where I couldn't really mess with the product because I didn't know how to edit or change code. I felt detached from my own product. 

Now, I can open up my own branch, do my own edits as the CTO, and feel confident that I am not messing up the codebase. I am then able to hand that back to the developers, and they can quickly integrate it into production. 

I think you should look for ways to foster that specific kind of relationship. Currently, you have two main groups:
1. The dev team using it as a tool to make them more productive.
2. Newbies starting out to build an MVP.

What you are missing is the "in-between." This is someone who isn't a technical founder but has significant industry knowledge. They can now go in and work on their product, platform, or software directly, feeling like they are working with their dev team rather than just having a meeting and waiting for them to make all the changes. It is quite a powerful tool.

column15: It's a coding agent, a proxy for a junior developer that runs in the cloud. Understands simple requirements, decomposes them into tasks and executes them. As opposed to Windsurf that is more of a local co-pilot/pair programmer that you can assign more fine-grained tasks to.
column18:
We had some success with Devin, for a few long process-oriented jobs. We were trying to overhaul our code and ran some repo-wide audits, wrote sweeping specs, etc. Devin helped with those.

Its offline nature and its ability to decompose (mostly simply stated) requirements was helpful. It was also useful for fixing some bugs. My co-founder also liked the end-to-end nature of how it uses playwright to do UI testing, etc.

However, there are some cases where it didn't work well for us, and that's a blocker.
1. It didn't do well at detail-oriented work - when we said "there are 3 tiles on page X and their titles/icons are not vertically aligned, please fix", it couldn't fix it even after 2 or 3 iterations. Maybe the difference between your default model with Devin (SWE-1.5?) and something like Opus/Sonnet 4.6?

2. It was never very fast - but over the few weeks we used it, it became VERY slow. We tried to pipeline tasks (and then had to deal with some merge conflicts) but for many tasks, we needed iterations (see #1 above) and the longer cycle times were annoying.

My baseline is using the latest Opus/Sonnet on Windsurf on my mac, which is FAST.

3. I tried to play cute - we have a bugfix leaderboard (buglord on slack) and I wanted to win. So I picked 8 very simple UI bugs and asked Devin to fix them. Maybe it was too many, maybe they should've been pipelined in different sessions. It didn't fix them all. The ones it claimed to fix were also not fixed correctly. I came away with the impression that it was not doing a detail-oriented job and I couldn't trust it. Sorry, guys.

4. This is not a small concern - Devin is VERY expensive compared to Windsurf even when we use 4x, 6x, 8x models. At that price point, it should just work the first time and well.

We're somewhat experienced programmers and to us, the additional control with Windsurf beats the hand-off nature of Devin. Perhaps, Devin is a better fit for someone looking for a junior dev. Please keep up the good work with Windsurf! We're big fans and power users!

Yes, I knocked a few points off the stability / reliability category for a couple persistent issues. For reference I use the JetBrains plugin (2.12.13) to interact with Windsurf.

The big one is at some point the memory system stopped working. It'll say it's creating memories, but they don't get stored anywhere. I poked around online and it seems like I'm not the only person having this problem.

The accept / reject changes part of the interface is pretty flakey. About half the time when I accept the changes it still shows outstanding changes in the files not matter how many times I click accept. And sometimes if I forget to click accept and move onto something else all the changes get lost so I have to spend tokens to recreate them.

I have the commands set to Auto Execution set to Turbo, but it doesn't always automatically run them. I haven't found a specific pattern that causes it.

It sometimes has issues with editing larger files. I will say this one has gotten a lot better, but it still happens occasionally where I need to make edits myself because it will fail at editing them with no explanation as to why.
I miss the "Split to right" button in the upper right corner of the Mac UI. I know there is a keyboard shortcut and you can drag but it was still nice.

column12: From some locations, I have trouble using the services. I expect it's some odd port that is blocked sometimes.

There really have been no new features added. The browser integration feature was removed and promises to replace it have not materialized. This should be a slam dunk to follow basic trends to enable features to enhance developer productivity that are being introduced in open code and claude code and enable first class support for skills and subagents and plugins along with a cloud hosted agent pool that runs continuously with access to repos and smallish set of tools/integrations with secure credential management but I see no vision or direction being communicated.

I am close to cancelling my subscription as the other offerings including antigravity and open code and kilo code are just better even at double the cost.

Please get a clear roadmap that includes first class browser integration, agent teams, and skills management along with some good, better, best support of continuously running agents
